1: Chapter 7 continues Job's address, shifting from the friends to a direct address of God beginning at v7. The chapter opens with one of Job's most powerful existential statements: 'does not man have ṣāḇāʾ on earth?' — ṣāḇāʾ means hard military service/forced labor (Numbers 4:3; Job 31:14; 2 Chronicles 17:18). The same term is used in Isaiah 40:2 ('her hard service [ṣāḇāʾ] is completed') for Babylon's completion of exile. This word choice frames human existence not as peaceful creature-life but as a tour of duty — exhausting, obligatory, under command — with a fixed term and a promised relief. The hireling (śāḵîr) simile of v2 develops the labor metaphor.
2: The hired worker (śāḵîr) is a specific social category in ancient Israel: the day-laborer who depends on daily wages for survival (Leviticus 19:13 'do not hold back the wages of a hired worker overnight'; Deuteronomy 24:14-15 on same-day payment requirements; James 5:4 condemning withholding wages). The śāḵîr longing for the shade (ṣēl) and watching for wages (yiqweh p̄ŏʿullāṯô) describes the specific experience of agricultural day-labor: the sun is the enemy, shade is rest, and wages are the only point of endurance. Job's life feels like this work — sustained only by the possibility of eventual payment/relief.
3: 'Months of futility' (yārĕḥê-šāwʾ = months of emptiness/vanity) — šāwʾ (emptiness/nothingness/vanity) is the same word used in the third commandment and in Job 31:5; its application to entire months gives Job's suffering a temporal expanse. 'Wearisome nights assigned to me' (lēlôṯ ʿāmāl sippĕr-lî = nights of trouble are counted out for me) — sāpar in the nip'al = to be assigned/meted out; the nights are not random but assigned by divine decree, making the exhaustion purposive rather than accidental.
4: The insomnia complaint (šāḵabû wāʾōmar = I lie down and say; mātay ʾāqûm = when will I rise?) reflects the ancient understanding that night brought either rest or terror. The Psalms frequently reference nighttime terror and insomnia as part of divine abandonment: Psalms 6:6 ('all night I flood my bed with weeping'); Psalms 22:2 ('by night I cry out and am not silent'); Job 30:5 ('weeping may stay for the night, but rejoicing comes in the morning' — the positive version). Job's nights extend toward dawn (wĕnešep̄ = twilight/dawn) with tossing (nidûḏ = restlessness) rather than resolution.
5: The physical description of Job's skin disease: 'covered with worms and clods of earth' (rimmāh wĕgeṯ-ʿāpār = worms and clods of dust) and 'skin cracks and oozes' (ʿôrî rāgaʿ wayyimmāʾēs = my skin hardens and flows again). The description fits severe dermatitis or the later-identified condition of Job as involving boils (Job 2:7), which in ancient medicine could indeed lead to secondary infection with larvae. The medical precision is notable; the description serves both to document the physical reality and to establish that Job's complaints are grounded in observable bodily suffering, not emotional exaggeration.
6: 'My days are swifter than a weaver's shuttle' (yāmay qallû miṇ-ʾōreḡ = my days are faster than a shuttle) — the shuttle (ʾōreḡ) that passes back and forth across the loom at great speed is a metaphor for the brevity of human life found also in Isaiah 38:12 (Hezekiah's lament: 'like a weaver I have rolled up my life, and he has cut me from the loom'). 'Without hope' (wĕyiḵlû ḇĕʾēn tiqwāh = they end without hope) — tiqwāh (hope) appears here at the very end of the verse, its absence the defining quality of the days' passing. The shuttle goes fast and arrives nowhere.
7: 'Remember that my life is but a breath' — Job's direct address to God begins: zĕḵōr kî-rûaḥ ḥayyāy (remember that a breath is my life). The imperative zĕḵōr (remember!) directed at God is a characteristic move in Hebrew lament: Psalms 25:7; Job 74:2; Job 89:47,50; Lamentations 3:19 all use it to call God to action on the basis of covenant memory. The breath-of-life (rûaḥ ḥayyāy) echoes Genesis 2:7 where God breathed the breath of life (nišmaṯ ḥayyîm) into the creature; Job's breath is the same divine breath now running short. 'My eyes will never again see happiness' (lōʾ-tāšûḇ ʿênî lirʾôṯ ṭôḇ = my eyes will not return to see good) — the return-to-happiness (šûḇ + ṭôḇ) is the standard formula for restoration; Job denies its applicability.
8: 'The eye of whoever sees me will see me no more' — the verse describes the irreversibility of death from the perspective of social relationships: the one who currently sees Job (rōʾēnî) will look for him (tĕšûrēnî = you will look carefully) but find him gone. The social-death aspect of Job's dying is its disappearance from the web of relationships. 'You will look for me but I will be gone' echoes the wisdom tradition's statements about the disappearance of the wicked (Psalms 37:36; 10 'I looked but he was gone') — but Job applies this dissolution vocabulary to himself, one of the righteous.
9: 'As a cloud vanishes and is gone, so the one who descends to Sheol does not come up' — three images of irreversibility: the vanishing cloud (ʿānān kālāh wĕyēleḵ = a cloud consumes itself and goes), the descent to Sheol (šĕʾôl yōrēḏ = the one going down to Sheol), and the non-ascent (lō-yaʿăleh = does not come up). Sheol in the OT is generally the undifferentiated realm of the dead — not hell but the shadowy underworld where the dead dwell as rĕp̄āʾîm (shades). The one-way downward direction dominates: Job's point is that death reversal is impossible, setting up his resurrection question in Job 14:14.
10: 'He will never return home, and his place will know him no more' — the dead man's 'place' (mĕqômô = his position in the household and community) no longer recognizes him; the familiar social slot is vacated permanently. The vocabulary of place-knowing/not-knowing (wĕlō-yakkîrennû ʿôḏ mĕqômô = his place will no longer recognize him) echoes the legal reality of social identity: a person's place in the community is their standing, property rights, and family relationships — all of which evaporate at death. Psalms 103:16 uses the same image: 'his place will know him no more.'
11: 'Therefore I will not hold back' — lāḵēn gam-ʾănî lōʾ ʾaḥśōḵ (therefore even I will not restrain myself). The logical transition: the irreversibility of death + the current suffering = the necessity of speaking now. 'I will speak in the anguish of my spirit and complain in the bitterness of my soul' — ʾăḏabbĕrāh bĕṣar rûḥî ʾāśîaḥ bĕmar nap̄šî. The vocabulary of anguish (ṣar rûaḥ = distress of spirit) and bitterness (mar nepeš = bitter soul; used in 1 Sam 1:10 of Hannah; Ruth 1:20 of Naomi at her lowest) places Job in the company of those whose extreme suffering authorized extreme expression before God.
12: 'Am I the sea, or a sea monster, that you place a guard over me?' — hayam-ʾănî ʾim-tannîn kî-ṯāśem ʿālay mišmār. This verse directly invokes Canaanite creation mythology. In the Ugaritic Baal Cycle, Yam (the sea) is the chaotic, hostile deity whom Baal must defeat to establish order; in Babylonian mythology, Tiamat is the sea-chaos monster whom Marduk battles. YHWH's 'guard' over the sea connects to Genesis 1:9-10 (God gathering the waters within their bounds) and Jeremiah 5:22 ('I placed sand as the boundary for the sea'). Job's ironic self-comparison to Yam/tannîn (sea monster) asks: am I a cosmic threat to be contained? Why does God guard me so closely if I am just a man?
13: The irony of the bed: 'when I say, my bed will comfort me, my couch will ease my complaint' — wĕʾāmartî tĕnaḥămēnî ʿarśî yiśśāʾ bĕśiḥî miškāḇî (my couch will lift up my complaint). The bed is the natural place of comfort and sleep in the ancient world; Psalms 4:4 ('commune with your own heart on your bed and be silent') and Psalms 63:6 ('I remember you on my bed') present the bed as a place of communion with God. Job has lost even this: the bed that should comfort terrifies (v14).
14: 'You terrify me with dreams and frighten me with visions' — wĕḥittaṯannî baḥălōmôṯ ûmēḥezyōnôṯ tĕḇahălannî. Dreams and visions in the ancient world were the primary channel of divine communication (Genesis 28:12 Jacob's dream; 1 Kgs 3:5 Solomon's dream; Dan 7 Daniel's visions). The expectation was that dreams brought divine messages; for the dreams to bring terror rather than guidance is another form of divine perversion of normal covenant channels. Eliphaz received a terrifying nocturnal vision as the basis for his opening argument (Job 4:12-17) — Job now reports the same phenomenon as pure torment rather than revelation.
15: 'So that I would choose strangulation and death rather than this suffering in my body' — wayyiḇḥar maḥănaq nap̄šî māwet mēʿaṣmôṯāy = my soul chooses strangulation, death, rather than my bones. The preference for strangulation over continued existence is the most extreme expression of Job's death-wish in the book; it goes beyond the rhetorical 'let me die' of Job 6 to specifying the means of death preferred. Strangulation (maḥănaq, from ḥānaq = to strangle; Nahum 2:12 uses it for the lion strangling prey) was not a normal mode of death but represented a swift, complete end. In context it reveals the depths of Job's physical and psychological suffering.
16: 'I am through with it; I would not live forever' — māʾastî lōʾ-lĕʿōlām ʾeḥyeh (I have rejected it; I will not live forever). The renunciation of eternal life as a good would be theologically scandalous in most OT contexts — long life is among God's greatest blessings (Psalms 91:16; Proverbs 3:16). Job's rejection of it expresses that the quality of existence has made its continuation undesirable. 'Leave me be, for my days are only a breath' — ḥaḏal mimmennî kî-heḇel yāmāy = leave off from me for my days are vapor/breath. The ḥeḇel (vapor/breath/vanity) of Ecclesiastes' world-view appears here as Job's self-assessment: his life is the emptiness Qohelet analyzes.
17: 'What is man, that you should make so much of him?' — mah-ʾĕnôš kî tĕgaḏlĕlennû = what is a mortal that you would magnify him? This is a deliberate inversion of Psalms 8:4 ('what is man that you are mindful of him, and the son of man that you care for him?'). In Psalm 8, the question is asked in wonder and gratitude: human beings are surprisingly honored given their cosmic insignificance. Job inverts the emotional valence: the same divine attention that Psalm 8 celebrates as gracious, Job experiences as oppressive over-surveillance. The NT trajectory: Hebrews 2:6-8 quotes Psalms 8:4-6 and applies it to Christ as the one in whom human dignity is fully realized; the gap between Job's experience of divine over-attention and Psalm 8's praise of it is bridged only in the incarnation.
18: 'Testing him every morning, examining him every moment' — wattip̄qĕḏennû liḇqārîm = you visit/examine him every morning; lirgāʿîm tiḇḥānenû = every moment you test him. The double-temporal structure (morning + moment) describes total temporal surveillance. In the OT, divine examination in the morning is associated with both blessing (Psalms 5:3 'in the morning I lay my requests before you'; Lamentations 3:23 'his mercies are new every morning') and judgment (Exodus 7:15 'go to Pharaoh in the morning'). For Job, the morning visit that should bring mercies brings only renewed scrutiny and pain.
19: 'How long will you not look away from me?' — mah-tišʿeh mimmenî = how long will you look away from me? The syntax is ambiguous: does Job want divine attention or want it to stop? Compare Job 7:7 ('remember me') with v19 ('stop looking at me'). The tension captures the paradox of God-experience in suffering: the God who is present but only as surveillance is worse than absence, yet absence would mean abandonment. 'Long enough to swallow' (ʿaḏ-bilʿî rōqî = until I swallow my saliva) — the extreme body-language of momentary relief: even a second's break to swallow would be something.
20: 'If I have sinned, what have I done to you, O watcher of mankind?' — ḥāṭāʾṯî mah-ʾep̄ʿal lāḵ nōṣēr hāʾāḏām (what have I done to you, watcher/guardian of humanity?). The divine epithet nōṣēr hāʾāḏām (guardian of mankind) is two-edged: a guardian normally protects, but this guardian is experienced as oppressive. 'Why have you made me your target?' (lāmmāh śamtannî lĕmip̄gāʿ lāḵ = why have you set me as a target for yourself?) — mip̄gāʿ is the target for an arrow (from pāgaʿ = to strike/meet; in military contexts the point of attack). Job as God's archery target connects back to Job 6:4's arrows of Shaddai — the consistent military metaphor frames Job's suffering as God conducting warfare against his servant.
21: 'Why do you not pardon my offense and forgive my sin?' — wĕlāmmāh lōʾ-tiśśāʾ pišʿî = why do you not lift up/carry my transgression? nāśāʾ ʿāwōn (to carry away iniquity) is the standard OT vocabulary for divine forgiveness: the same phrase appears in Exodus 34:7 (YHWH 'carrying iniquity, transgression, and sin'), Psalms 32:5, and Micah 7:18 ('who is a God like you, who pardons sin and forgives transgression?'). Job's final verse uses precisely this forgiveness vocabulary — not as a confession of guilt but as a question: why not use your own stated capacity for forgiveness now, before I die? 'For soon I shall lie down in the dust' — the urgency of mortality: forgiveness deferred past death is forgiveness that cannot be received.
